coredata.dc:title: Enhanced phthalocyanine-sensitized solar cell efficiency via cooperation of nitrogen-doped carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Ghadari, Rahim
Saei, Paria-Sadat
Sabri, Alireza
Ghasemi, Zarrin
Kong, Fantai
coredata.dc:description: Nitrogen-containing carbon dots were applied to improve the performance of phthalocyanine-sensitized solar cells. Nitrogen-doped carbon dots were prepared by carbonizing the citric acid as a carbon source in water due to their green and environmental friendliness properties. Two sets of dye systems consisting of phthalocyanine-carbon dots conjugates and phthalocyanine-doped carbon dots have been provided to elucidate the influence of nitrogen-doped carbon dots on the performance of devices. The synthesis of phthalocyanines and derived metal complexes was done using the controlled microwave heating method as a green procedure. The good biocompatibility and excellent water solubility of citric acid-based carbon dots in combination with green manufacturing of phthalocyanines make the prepared dye systems appropriate to be used as green sensitizing systems in solar cells. The phthalocyanine-carbon dots conjugate systems were prepared by mixing the related phthalocyanines and nitrogen-doped carbon dots, while the phthalocyanine-doped carbon dots were prepared by in situ synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon dots in the presence of the related phthalocyanines. Metal-free phthalocyanines and metalophthalocyanines (metal: manganese, iron, cobalt, nickel, copper, and zinc) were prepared and examined to show the effect of the metal in the core of phthalocyanine on the performance of devices. Also, un-substituted and carboxyl- and sulfo-substituted derivatives of phthalocyanines were studied in both sets of dye systems. The obtained results confirmed the significant influence of nitrogen-containing carbon dots on the performance of the solar cells.

head.ce:title: Enhanced phthalocyanine-sensitized solar cell efficiency via cooperation of nitrogen-doped carbon dots

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nitrogen-containing carbon dots were applied to improve the performance of phthalocyanine-sensitized solar cells. Nitrogen-doped carbon dots were prepared by carbonizing the citric acid as a carbon source in water due to their green and environmental friendliness properties. Two sets of dye systems consisting of phthalocyanine-carbon dots conjugates and phthalocyanine-doped carbon dots have been provided to elucidate the influence of nitrogen-doped carbon dots on the performance of devices. The synthesis of phthalocyanines and derived metal complexes was done using the controlled microwave heating method as a green procedure. The good biocompatibility and excellent water solubility of citric acid-based carbon dots in combination with green manufacturing of phthalocyanines make the prepared dye systems appropriate to be used as green sensitizing systems in solar cells. The phthalocyanine-carbon dots conjugate systems were prepared by mixing the related phthalocyanines and nitrogen-doped carbon dots, while the phthalocyanine-doped carbon dots were prepared by in situ synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon dots in the presence of the related phthalocyanines. Metal-free phthalocyanines and metalophthalocyanines (metal: manganese, iron, cobalt, nickel, copper, and zinc) were prepared and examined to show the effect of the metal in the core of phthalocyanine on the performance of devices. Also, un-substituted and carboxyl- and sulfo-substituted derivatives of phthalocyanines were studied in both sets of dye systems. The obtained results confirmed the significant influence of nitrogen-containing carbon dots on the performance of the solar cells.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, numerous studies have focused on solar light-harvesting materials and wide bandgap semiconductors because of the remarkable properties of dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs) (Amiri et al., 2016; Mathew et al., 2014; Sarkar et al., 2012; Shahid et al., 2013). The solar light harvesting in DSSCs is done using the sensitizing dye attached to a wide bandgap semiconductor (Reijnders, 2010). The DSSCs first introduced by O’Regan and Grätzel that opening a new field for researchers in sustainable energy supply with the prospect of developing photovoltaic devices at low fabrication cost and high environmental friendliness (Grätzel, 2003; O’Regan and Grätzel, 1991). The sensitizing dye is an essential constituent in DSSCs and the most successful dyes in DSSCs are ruthenium complexes due to their broad optical absorption spectra (Grätzel, 2003). However, up to date, relatively low molar extinction coefficients of ruthenium-based dyes and their high synthesis cost limit their further applications in DSSCs. Therefore, introducing sensitizing dye materials with higher molar extinction coefficients into DSSCs is an important alternative way to fabricate DSSCs (Grätzel, 2003). Alternative organic dyes have higher molar extinction coefficients compared to ruthenium-based dyes, but their narrow spectral bandwidths limit them in DSSCs applications. The focusing on the development of high-performance sensitizers to enhance light-absorbing in the most photon rich part of sunlight is considered as a favorable approach to improve the efficiency of the DSSCs (Gong et al., 2017). Phthalocyanines (Pcs) are two-dimensional tetrapyrrolic macroheterocycles with 18 delocalized π-electrons in their structure. The intense Soret- and Q-band absorptions of Pcs occur in UV–Vis and the red/near-infrared region of the sunlight, respectively (Kadish et al., 2012; Sorokin, 2013; Urbani et al., 2019). Since the red and NIR spectral regions are the most photon rich part of the solar spectrum, Pcs are considered as ideal light-harvesting dyes in the sensitization of the solar cell (Urbani et al., 2019). The Pcs have advantages of high molar extinction coefficients, rich redox chemistry, and intense absorption, which may lead to the construction of a designable and tunable light-harvesting platform for DSSCs applications (Kadish et al., 2012). The intensity of absorptivity and spectral bandwidths are among the major drawbacks of using dyes as photosensitizers in solar cells. Many efforts have been made to introduce light-harvesting materials with stronger light absorptivity and/or broaden light absorption. Most recently, the use of quantum dots (QDs) as light harvesters in the sensitized solar cells has been developed due to the unique and tunable optical and electronic properties of the semiconductor QDs (Engül and Theis, 2011; Ming et al., 2020). Carbon quantum dots (CDs) offer an alternative to traditional semiconductor inorganic QDs for optoelectronic applications that taking advantage of carbon; consisting of low cost, high stability, environment-friendliness, and high abundance (Yuan et al., 2018). Thus, great attention has been paid to investigate various methods for the synthesis of CDs that among them, the carbonization of eco-friendly carbon-based material is an outstanding method to produce CDs from green chemistry viewpoint (Ren et al., 2019; Yang et al., 2019, 2014). Citric acid as a simple, low-cost, and water-soluble compound is a good candidate to prepare CDs by carbonizing method due to its green and environmental friendliness properties. Citric acid-based carbon dots are a class of advanced semiconductor nanostructured materials that are becoming ever more favored for their good biocompatibility, excellent water solubility, good optical stability, and good thermal and electrical conductivity (Dong et al., 2012; Krysmann et al., 2012). Additionally, owing to their capability of functionalization, citric acid-based precursors are more appropriate to be used in the preparation of doped-carbon dots. Citric acid as a carbon source and nitrogen-containing precursors as nitrogen source are good candidates to synthesize nitrogen-doped (N-doped) CDs (Dong et al., 2012; Krysmann et al., 2012). As a result, citric acid-based NCDs are environmentally benign alternatives to QDs comprised of heavy metals to be used in solar cells. To access the efficiency and sustainability during the synthesis of NCDs, consideration of the possibility of commercial production at large scale and the potential environmental impacts generated by different strategies will be important. The significant energy- and material-consuming of the production of nanomaterials compared to non-nano analogous have been identified as an environmental concern about QDs. The studies about CDs prepared from citric acid have shown that the energy and chemicals used during the synthesis of citric acid-based carbon dots contribute a significant share of the environmental impacts generated during the manufacturing of CDs (Christé et al., 2020; Engül and Theis, 2011; Ludin et al., 2018). The potential of commercial production at the large scale of citric acid-based CDs from the simple, green, and biocompatible precursor, recyclability at end-of-life, the embodied energy of pyrolysis, and the waste and emissions of the process are another green aspects of manufacturing of citric acid-based CDs. Therefore, in this work, NCDs are used to improve the light absorption of dye molecules in phthalocyanine-sensitized solar cells (Pc-SSCs). Two sets of dye systems consisting of phthalocyanine-NCDs conjugates (MTXPc-NCDs) and phthalocyanine-doped NCDs ((MTXPc)NCDs) were prepared and applied to examine the influence of NCDs on the performance of the Pc-SSCs. To study the effect of NCDs on the performance of Pc-SSCs, phthalocyanine derivatives with various metal in the core of the phthalocyanine ring and different substitutions are prepared and applied to sensitize the solar cells. Metal-free phthalocyanine and manganese-, iron-, cobalt-, nickel-, copper-, and zinc-phthalocyanine are selected to be used in Pc-SSCs to study the effect of the metal in the core of phthalocyanine ring in interaction with NCDs on the efficiency of the solar cells. The effect of the anchoring groups on the phthalocyanine in interaction with NCDs was investigated on the performance of Pc-SSCs using carboxylic and sulfonic functional groups in comparison with unsubstituted ones.

1,2,4-Benzenetricarboxylic acid (C9H6O6, ≥99.0%), urea (CH4N2O, ≥99.0%), phthalic anhydride (C8H4O3, ≥98.0%), metal(II) sulfate, metal(II) chloride, ammonium heptamolybdate (H24Mo7N6O24, ≥99.3%), ammonium sulfate (H8N2O4S, ≥99.5%), chlorosulfonic acid (HClO3S, ≥97.0%), citric acid (C6H8O7, ≥99.5%), terpineol (C10H18O, ≥80.0%), tetrapropyl orthotitanat (C12H28O4Ti, ≥ 98.0%), and ethyl cellulose were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich and Merck. All solvents (1,2,4-trichlorobenzene, methanol, ethanol, acetone, acetonitrile, and tert-butanol) were obtained from Aldrich or Merck and used without further purification. FTO substrates, Pt paste, and standard iodine-based electrolyte were obtained from SharifSolar (IRASOL). Surlyn was purchased from SUNLAB. Titanium oxide nanopowder (TiO2, anatase, 99+%, 10–25 nm) was obtained from US Reasearch Nanomaterials, Inc.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of phthalocyanine (MPc), tetracarboxy phthalocyanine (MTCPc), and zinc tetrasulfophthalocyanine (ZnTSPc)[M = H2, Mn, Fe, Co, Ni, Cu, and Zn]
ce:section.ce:para: The phthalocyanine (MPc), tetracarboxy phthalocyanine (MTCPc) and zinc tetrasulfophthalocyanine (ZnTSPc)[M = H2, Mn, Fe, Co, Ni, Cu, and Zn] are synthesized using the previously reported methods (Hezarkhani et al., 2017; Hezarkhani and Shaabani, 2016; Shaabani and Hezarkhani, 2016, 2015). Briefly, the phthalocyanine derivatives were synthesized using the controlled microwave heating method (Scheme 1 ). The starting materials were grounded together to obtain homogeneous powder before the irradiation by microwave. 1,2,4-benzenetricarboxylic acid (1.26 g, 6.0 mmol), urea (1.6 g, 26.0 mmol), metal(II) sulfate or metal(II) chloride (1.8 mmol), and ammonium heptamolybdate (0.02 g, 0.02 mmol) are grounded together and then irradiated by microwave to obtain MTCPc (M = Mn, Fe, Co, Ni, Cu, and Zn). The H2TCPc was synthesized by a similar method without metal salt. Metalo-phthalocyanines (MPc; M = Mn, Fe, Co, Ni, Cu, and Zn) and metal-free phthalocyanine (H2Pc) were synthesized in the presence of phthalic anhydride (0.88 g, 6.0 mmol) instead of 1,2,4-benzenetricarboxylic acid. The synthesized ZnPc (1.0 mmol) was sulfonated using chlorosulfonic acid (7.0 mmol). The sulfonation of ZnPc was carried on during 2 h in 1,2,4-trichlorobenzene (50 ml) at 180–190 °C to afford ZnTSPc (Scheme 1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of nitrogen doped carbon dots (NCDs), phthalocyanine-NCDs conjugates (MTXPc-NCDs), and phthalocyanine doped NCDs ((MTXPc)NCDs)
ce:section.ce:para: The NCDs were synthesized by direct pyrolyzing of citric acid (as carbon source) and ammonia (as nitrogen source) according to the reported procedure (Wang et al., 2016). In brief, citric acid (5.2 mmol) was dissolved in aqueous ammonia solution (0.05 mmol of ammonia in 2.5 mL distilled water) with magnetically stirring for 30 min. The obtained solution was heated in a muffle furnace in the air by the heating rate of 10 °C/min to reach 200 °C. The sample was maintained at 200 °C for about 3 h. The cooled solid product was mixed with acetone by ultrasonication. After centrifugation at 10,000 rpm for 15 min, the supernatants were collected. The final NCDs were obtained by rotary evaporation of acetone of the supernatants.
The phthalocyanine-NCDs conjugates (MTXPc-NCDs) were prepared by mixing the related phthalocyanines (0.1 mmol) and the as-prepared NCDs (Scheme 2 ).
The phthalocyanine doped NCDs ((MTXPc)NCDs) were prepared with a similar procedure as used for the NCDs by mixing the prepared MTXPc (0.1 mmol) and citric acid in aqueous ammonia solution (Scheme 2).

The photo-anode was fabricated by previously established reports (Chu et al., 2015; Ding et al., 2011; Góes et al., 2012; Wooh et al., 2013). The transparent and reflector TiO2 pastes were prepared by sonicating a mixture of TiO2 NPs, ethyl cellulose, terpinol, and acetic acid in ethanol. TiO2 NPs with the size of 20 nm and 300 nm were used to afford the transparent and reflector TiO2 pastes, respectively. Fluorine-doped tin oxide (FTO) glass substrates (15 Ω/cm2) were cleaned by sonicating in DI water, HCl (0.1 M), acetone, and ethanol baths sequentially for 20 min. The FTO glasses were dried under N2 gas flow. The cleaned substrates were immersed in titanium isopropoxide (Ti(i-pro)4, 40 mM) solution in a beaker and then kept in an oil bath at 50 °C for 30 min to form a compact TiO2 layer, followed by rinsing with DI water and methanol and drying. The transparent and reflector TiO2 pastes were printed onto the Ti(i-pro)4-treated FTO substrates by doctor-blading method, respectively, and the TiO2 nanoparticles pastes were allowed to dry at room temperature for 15 min. Then, the TiO2 layers were sintered in a muffle furnace under airflow at 125 °C (15 min), 325 °C (5 min), 375 °C (5 min), 450 °C (15 min), and 500 °C (30 min). Before dye adsorption, TiO2 nanoparticles films were once again immersed in 40 mM Ti(i-pro)4 solution in a beaker and then kept in an oil bath at 50 °C for 30 min, followed by rinsing with DI water and methanol and annealing at 500 °C in air for 30 min. Subsequently, the surface-treated TiO2 nanoparticles film was cooled to 80 °C and immersed in a 0.5 mM dye solution in a mixture of acetonitrile/tert-butanol (volume ratio, 1:1) for 16 h to allow for sufficient dye adsorption.

Platinum (Pt)-coated FTO glass was used as the counter electrode, prepared by printing the Pt paste on a clean FTO glass substrate with drilled holes by the doctor blade method. Subsequently, the Pt layer was sintered at 460 °C for 15 min in a muffle furnace under an air atmosphere. DSSCs were sandwiched between the working electrode (TiO2 nanoparticles paste-coated FTO glass) and the counter electrode (Pt-coated FTO glass) by applying a 50 μm thick thermoplastic spacer (Surlyn) with an open area of 0.25 cm2. The working and counter electrodes were sealed together by heating at 120 °C during 90 s. An iodine-based electrolyte, containing iodine, lithium iodide, 1,2-dimethyl-3-propylimidazolium iodide, and 4-tert-butylpyridine in acetonitrile was injected between two electrodes. Finally, the holes were sealed with a piece of the Surlyn spacer.

The optical absorption characteristics of the prepared samples were evaluated using a Shimadzu UV-2100 spectrophotometer. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) observations were carried out on a scanning electron microscope (TESCAN Vega Model). All samples were sputtered with gold before observation. Photovoltaic measurements were performed under illumination generated by an AM 1.5 solar simulator (Fan Daghigh Kosar, SIM2002) with a 300 W xenon lamp and an air-mass 1.5 global filter. The power of the simulated light was calibrated to 100 mW/cm2 by using a reference silicon solar cell. The J-V curves were obtained by applying an external bias to the cell and measuring the generated photocurrent with a Fan Daghigh Kosar model IV2000 digital source meter. The voltage step and delay time of the photocurrent were 30 mV and 1 s, respectively.

The synthesis of the NCDs was generally involved the direct pyrolyzing of citric acid and ammonia. Citric acid and ammonia are used as carbon and nitrogen source, respectively. The qualitative determination of the elemental composition of the NCDs was evaluated using an energy dispersive X-ray spectrometer (EDS) (Fig. 1 ). The presence C, N, and O signified the successful formation of the NCDs. In the synthesis of phthalocyanine doped NCDs, the direct pyrolyzing of citric acid and ammonia was done in the presence of the related phthalocyanine. EDS analysis was also applied to evaluate the qualitative determination of the elemental composition of the prepared phthalocyanine doped NCDs. The results of the EDS analysis of the (H2Pc)NCDs, (MnPc)NCDs, (FePc)NCDs, (CoPc)NCDs, (NiPc)NCDs, (CuPc)NCDs, (ZnPc)NCDs, (MnTCPc)NCDs, (CoTCPc)NCDs, (NiTCPc)NCDs, (CuTCPc)NCDs, and (ZnTCPc)NCDs are presented in Fig. 1 and S1 (electronic supplementary information (ESI)). The obtained results were consistent with the expected elemental composition for the mentioned phthalocyanine doped NCDs. The presence C, N, and O in all of the prepared phthalocyanine doped NCDs and the presence of Mn, Fe, Co, Ni, Cu, and Zn for (MnPc)NCDs and (MnTCPc)NCDs, (FePc)NCDs and (FeTCPc)NCDs, (CoPc)NCDs and (CoTCPc)NCDs, (NiPc)NCDs and (NiTCPc)NCDs, (CuPc)NCDs and (CuTCPc)NCDs, and (ZnPc)NCDs and (ZnTCPc)NCDs, respectively, signified the successful formation of the mentioned structures.

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) equipped with EDS mapping was used for elemental microanalysis of NCDs, (H2Pc)NCDs, (MnPc)NCDs, (CoPc)NCDs, (NiPc)NCDs, (CuPc)NCDs, (ZnPc)NCDs, (H2TCPc)NCDs, (MnTCPc)NCDs, (FeTCPc)NCDs, (CoTCPc)NCDs, (NiTCPc)NCDs, (CuTCPc)NCDs, and (ZnTCPc)NCDs (Figs. S2–S15, ESI). SEM equipped with EDS element mapping clearly showed the elements C and N evenly distributed in NCDs, (H2Pc)NCDs, and (H2TCPc)NCDs materials (Figs. S2–S4, ESI). The obtained data confirmed the presence of carbon and nitrogen in all of the (MPc)NCDs and (MTCPc)NCDs. The resulted data indicated the existence of Mn, Co, Ni, Cu, and Zn homogeneously in the structures of the (MnPc)NCDs, (CoPc)NCDs, (NiPc)NCDs, (CuPc)NCDs, and (ZnPc)NCDs, respectively (Figs. S5–S9, ESI). The homogeneous dispersion of Mn, Fe, Co, Ni, Cu, and Zn in the structures of the (MnTCPc)NCDs, (FeTCPc)NCDs, (CoTCPc)NCDs, (NiTCPc)NCDs, (CuTCPc)NCDs, and (ZnTCPc)NCDs was also confirmed by SEM equipped with EDS mapping, respectively (Figs. S10–S15, ESI).

UV–Vis spectroscopic investigations have been undertaken to understand the photophysical behaviors of the prepared systems. The UV–Vis absorption spectra of the phthalocyanines and phthalocyanine-doped NCDs are shown in Fig. S16 and S17 (ESI). In the UV–Vis spectrum of NCDs, the absorption peak at around 350 nm is observable because of n-π∗ transition in the carbon dots (Barman et al., 2014). The Soret- and Q-band regions are observed in about 300–400 and 600–800 nm for the phthalocyanines and phthalocyanine-doped NCDs, respectively (Sorokin, 2013). The Q-band absorptions for the phthalocyanines and phthalocyanine-doped NCDs are showing that there are no significant shifts in the Q-band of the phthalocyanines upon interaction with NCDs. As it is clear from Figs. S16 and S17 (ESI), the intense Q-band absorption of phthalocyanines and phthalocyanine-doped NCDs is observable in the red/near-infrared region of the solar spectrum that is the most photon rich part of sunlight (Urbani et al., 2019). The high thermal and chemical stability and optical properties of phthalocyanines make them good candidates to be used as a sensitizer in the solar cells (Kadish et al., 2012). Also, the rich redox chemistry of phthalocyanines is suitable for the electronic processes that occur in the solar cells (Sorokin, 2013). Since the redox chemistry of phthalocyanines can be tuned by their metal center, in this work, phthalocyanines with various metals as manganese, iron, cobalt, nickel, copper, and zinc are used to interact with NCDs and the prepared systems are used to sensitize the solar cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of NCDs on the performance of Pc-SSCs
ce:section.ce:para:
Current-voltage characteristics of the phthalocyanine-sensitized solar cells in the presence of NCDs, phthalocyanine-NCDs conjugates, and phthalocyanine-doped NCDs systems in simulated sunlight are shown in Figs. 2–5
and the cell parameters are summarized in Tables 1–4
. Also, the experimental data points including error bars are provided in the graphs of Figs. S18–S21 (ESI).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of NCDs on the performance of Pc-SSCs using phthalocyanine-NCDs conjugate systems
ce:section.ce:para: To better study the effect of phthalocyanine doping in NCDs on the performance of the solar cells, the conjugate systems consisting of MPc and NCDs (MPc-NCDs) were prepared by the separate synthesis of MPc and NCDs and mixing of them. For this purpose, H2Pc, MnPc, FePc, CoPc, NiPc, CuPc, and ZnPc were selected. The conjugate systems consisting of H2Pc-NCDs, MnPc-NCDs, FePc-NCDs, CoPc-NCDs, NiPc-NCDs, CuPc-NCDs, and ZnPc-NCDs were prepared and used to sensitize the solar cells. The J-V characteristics of the solar cells sensitized by MPc-NCDs are presented in Fig. 2, and the cell parameters are summarized in Table 1. The solar cell sensitized by ZnPc-NCDs gives higher power conversion efficiency than the solar cell sensitized by ZnPc, as it gives higher photocurrent, photovoltage, and fill factor. The highly improved efficiency of the solar cell sensitized by ZnPc-NCDs compared to ZnPc is confirming the significant influence of NCDs on the performance of Pc-SSCs. From the obtained data in Fig. 2 and Table 1, the ZnPc-NCDs showed the best efficiency relative to other systems and the H2Pc-NCDs was the worst one, which confirmed the role of the metal in the core of phthalocyanine on the performance of Pc-SSCs.

The low solubility of the phthalocyanines such as H2Pc, MnPc, FePc, CoPc, NiPc, CuPc, and ZnPc in water is the most important limitation of the suggested method to prepare MPc-NCDs conjugate systems and MPc-doped NCDs. By using the functionalized phthalocyanines such as H2TCPc, MnTCPc, FeTCPc, CoTCPc, NiTCPc, CuTCPc, and ZnTCPc, this limitation significantly was overcame. Therefore, the MTCPc-NCDs (M = H2, Mn, Fe, Co, Ni, Cu, and Zn) conjugate systems were prepared and applied to sensitize the solar cells. The prepared conjugate systems contain H2TCPc-NCDs, MnTCPc-NCDs, FeTCPc-NCDs, CoTCPc-NCDs, NiTCPc-NCDs, CuTCPc-NCDs, and ZnTCPc-NCDs. The obtained results are presented in Fig. 3 and Table 2. The higher photocurrent, photovoltage, fill factor, and consequently, power conversion efficiency of the solar cell sensitized by ZnTCPc-NCDs compared to ZnTCPc confirmed the significant influence of NCDs in conjugation with phthalocyanine on the performance of Pc-SSCs. The overall efficiency of ZnTCPc-NCDs-sensitized solar cell was found to be higher relative to ZnPc-NCDs one, as the ZnPc dye did not have the anchoring groups. The presence of the carboxylic anchoring groups on ZnTCPc leads to the chemical attachment of dye to the NCDs and the semiconductor surface. Therefore, the amount of adsorbed ZnTCPc molecules through chemisorption will be increased. Since the substitution of Pcs with suitable anchoring groups lead to an increase in the amount of adsorbed dye, ZnTSPc was prepared and used to study the effect of anchoring groups on the performance of Pc-SSCs in the presence of NCDs (Fig. 3 and Table 2). Any ZnTSPc molecule has four sulfonic acid functional groups which can chemically be attached to the surface of the NCDs and the semiconductor. Also, the ZnTSPc phthalocyanine is completely soluble in water; therefore, the limitation of the solubility of ZnPc in the preparation of phthalocyanine-NCDs conjugate system and phthalocyanine-doped NCDs significantly was overcame. From the obtained data in Tables 1 and 2, the efficiency of Pc-SSCs using ZnTSPc-NCDs increased relative to ZnPc-NCDs. Therefore, the presence of anchoring groups on Pcs makes them more suitable for Pc-SSCs applications. Also, different substituents on phthalocyanines may be a cause for high efficiency because, by the substitution of Pcs, the bandwidth of their absorption could be shifted (Sorokin, 2013). To study the role of the metal in the core of Pcs on the performance of NCDs-enhanced Pc-SSCs, various phthalocyanines consisting of H2TCPc, MnTCPc, FeTCPc, CoTCPc, NiTCPc, CuTCPc, and ZnTCPc are applied in conjugation with NCDs to sensitize the solar cells. The J-V characteristics of the Pc-SSCs employing H2TCPc-NCDs, MnTCPc-NCDs, FeTCPc-NCDs, CoTCPc-NCDs, NiTCPc-NCDs, CuTCPc-NCDs, and ZnTCPc-NCDs are presented in Fig. 3, and the cell parameters are summarized in Table 2. The short-circuit current density (JSC), open-circuit voltage (VOC), fill factor (ff), and overall conversion efficiency (η) of the solar cell sensitized by H2TCPc-NCDs are 2.76 mA cm−2, 406 mV, 0.49, and 0.55%, respectively. For ZnTCPc-NCDs, the photovoltaic parameters are 4.20 mA cm−2, 470 mV, 0.51, and 1.01%, respectively. The obtained data is showing that using zinc metal in the core of Pcs, the overall conversion efficiency of the prepared cell is significantly increased relative to corresponding metal-free phthalocyanine, H2TCPc-NCDs. For CuTCPc-NCDs, the photovoltaic parameters are 3.09 mA cm−2, 440 mV, 0.48, and 0.66%, respectively. Compared to the photovoltaic parameters of H2TCPc-NCDs, the efficiency of the solar cell sensitized by CuTCPc-NCDs is slightly increased which confirms the role of the metal in the core of Pcs on the performance of NCDs-enhanced Pc-SSCs. In the case of the solar cell sensitized be CoTCPc-NCDs, the cell parameters containing JSC, VOC, ff, and η are 3.85 mA cm−2, 437 mV, 0.55, and 0.93%, respectively. From data, the improvement of the efficiency of the Pc-SSCs using CoTCPc-NCDs is placed in the second rank after ZnTCPc-NCDs relative to H2TCPc-NCDs. Using the NiTCPc-NCDs as a sensitizer, the efficiency of the solar cell is improved noticeably, but using MnTCPc-NCDs and FeTCPc-NCDs, the improvement is not significant.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of NCDs on the performance of Pc-SSCs using phthalocyanine-doped NCDs systems
ce:section.ce:para: To improve the effect of the Pcs-NCDs conjugates on the performance of the solar cells, the Pcs-doped NCDs were synthesized by adding the Pcs to the starting materials of NCDs. Therefore, NCDs were prepared in the presence of the Pcs to obtain phthalocyanine-doped NCDs. H2Pc, MnPc, FePc, CoPc, NiPc, CuPc, and ZnPc were selected and used to in situ synthesis of (MPc)NCDs and named (H2Pc)NCDs, (MnPc)NCDs, (FePc)NCDs, (CoPc)NCDs, (NiPc)NCDs, (CuPc)NCDs, and (ZnPc)NCDs, respectively. The prepared (MPc)NCDs were used to further investigate the effect of NCDs on the performance of Pc-SSCs (Fig. 4 and Table 3). The JSC, VOC, ff, and η of the solar cell sensitized by ZnPc-NCDs are 3.83 mA cm−2, 479 mV, 0.50, and 0.92%, respectively. While, the photovoltaic parameters of the solar cell employing (ZnPc)NCDs are 8.96 mA cm−2, 466 mV, 0.49, and 2.05%, respectively. As it is clear from obtained data, using zinc phthalocyanine doping in NCDs ((ZnPc)NCDs), the efficiency of the solar cell is significantly increased relative to correspond conjugate ZnPc-NCDs system. The resulted data confirmed the effect of phthalocyanine doping on the performance of the solar cell which is due to the uniform distribution of phthalocyanine molecules on the surface of the prepared NCDs. Compared to (ZnPc)NCDs, the photovoltaic parameters of the solar cell sensitized by (H2Pc)NCDs showed a noticeable decreasing in efficiency. It is confirming the critical role of the metal in the core of phthalocyanine on the performance of the solar cells. The result of employing H2Pc-NCDs as a sensitizer in the solar cell indicated that doped H2Pc in NCDs ((H2Pc)NCDs) performed better than conjugated one (H2Pc-NCDs). To further examine the effect of the metal in the core of Pcs on the performance of NCDs-enhanced Pc-SSCs, other metals consisting of Mn, Fe, Co, Ni, and Cu were selected and applied in the solar cells. The JSC, VOC, ff, and η of the solar cell sensitized by (NiPc)NCDs are 7.07 mA cm−2, 455 mV, 0.56, and 1.80%, respectively. The cell parameters using (CoPc)NCDs are 6.45 mA cm−2, 437 mV, 0.56, and 1.58%, respectively. Both of (NiPc)NCDs and (CoPc)NCDs systems led to improved efficiency of the prepared solar cells in considerable quantity compared to (H2Pc)NCDs. While the improvement in the performance of the solar cells employing (FePc)NCDs and (CuPc)NCDs systems was relatively less than (NiPc)NCDs and (CoPc)NCDs systems. By using (MnPc)NCDs, the photocurrent and the efficiency of the prepared cell increased in a noticeable amount relative to (H2Pc)NCDs. The changes in the cell characteristics comparing (ZnPc)NCDs and (H2Pc)NCDs systems relative to ZnPc-NCDs and H2Pc-NCDs confirmed the importance of Pcs-doping in NCDs on the performance of the solar cells. The JSC of the solar cells sensitized by H2Pc-NCDs, ZnPc-NCDs, (H2Pc)NCDs, and (ZnPc)NCDs was 2.63, 3.83, 3.78, 8.96 mA cm−2, respectively. As it is clear from data, using ZnPc-NCDs and (ZnPc)NCDs systems, the JSC is increased compared to metal-free phthalocyanine H2Pc-NCDs and (H2Pc)NCDs, respectively, but increasing in JSC of (ZnPc)NCDs relative to (H2Pc)NCDs is more considerable. The mentioned examinations reveal that NCDs are more efficient for co–sensitization of phthalocyanine dyes by doping of Pcs in the NCDs relative to that of conjugation. The in situ synthesis of NCDs in the presence of Pcs enabling the higher dispersion of the phthalocyanine dyes, followed by better co-sensitization of dyes using NCDs.

The photovoltaic parameters of the solar cells employing (NiPc)NCDs consisting of JSC, VOC, ff, and η are 7.07 mA cm−2, 455 mV, 0.56, and 1.80%, respectively. Compared to (H2Pc)NCDs, the JSC of the solar cell using (NiPc)NCDs is increased by 87% and η is increased from 0.77% to 1.80%. The cell parameters using NiPc-NCDs are 3.49 mA cm−2, 432 mV, 0.59, and 0.90%, respectively, in which the JSC of NiPc-NCDs is increased by 33% relative to H2Pc-NCDs. Also, the overall conversion efficiency of NiPc-NCDs and H2Pc-NCDs was 0.90% and 0.51%, respectively. The mentioned data is confirming the critical importance of phthalocyanine-doping relative to phthalocyanine-conjugation with NCDs in the performance of Pc-SSCs. To further examine the role of phthalocyanine-doping relative to phthalocyanine-conjugation with NCDs, the performance of the systems consisting (CoPc)NCDs, (MnPc)NCDs, (CuPc)NCDs, and (FePc)NCDs are compared with CoPc-NCDs, MnPc-NCDs, CuPc-NCDs, FePc-NCDs in the Pc-SSCs, respectively. The resulted data obtained by the solar cells sensitized by (CoPc)NCDs and CoPc-NCDs showed 71% and 28% increase in JSC compared to (H2Pc)NCDs and H2Pc-NCDs, respectively. In the solar cells employing (MnPc)NCDs and MnPc-NCDs, the JSC was increased by 63% and 19% relative to (H2Pc)NCDs and H2Pc-NCDs, respectively, which confirmed the role of phthalocyanine-doping in NCDs on the performance of the solar cells. The Pc-SSCs using the (CuPc)NCDs- and CuPc-NCDs-loaded TiO2 as the photoanodes showed the η of 0.81% and 0.65%, respectively. Also, the η in the cells employing (FePc)NCDs- and FePc-NCDs-loaded TiO2 as the photoanodes was about 0.90% and 0.63%, while (H2Pc)NCDs and H2Pc-NCDs systems showed the η of 0.77% and 0.51%, respectively.

To further compare the effect of the Pcs-NCDs conjugates and phthalocyanine doping in NCDs on the performance of the solar cells, the (MTCPc)NCDs (M = H2, Mn, Fe, Co, Ni, Cu, and Zn) were prepared and applied to sensitize the solar cells. The MTCPc-NCDs conjugate systems were prepared by mixing of MTCPc and NCDs, while phthalocyanine-doped NCDs, ((MTCPc)NCDs) were prepared by adding the corresponding MTCPc to the starting materials of NCDs. The prepared NCDs in the presence of the H2TCPc, MnTCPc, FeTCPc, CoTCPc, NiTCPc, CuTCPc, and ZnTCPc to obtain phthalocyanine-doped NCDs were named (H2TCPc)NCDs, (MnTCPc)NCDs, (FeTCPc)NCDs, (CoTCPc)NCDs, (NiTCPc)NCDs, (CuTCPc)NCDs, and (ZnTCPc)NCDs, respectively. The J-V characteristics of the solar cells sensitized by (MTCPc)NCDs are presented in Fig. 5, and the cell parameters are summarized in Table 4.

From the obtained data in Tables 2 and 4, increased JSC in the (ZnTCPc)NCDs and ZnTCPc-NCDs was about 213% and 52% compared to (H2TCPc)NCDs and H2TCPc-NCDs, respectively. The η is increased in a considerable amount from 0.80% to 0.55% in (H2TCPc)NCDs and H2TCPc-NCDs to 2.83% and 1.01% in (ZnTCPc)NCDs and ZnTCPc-NCDs, respectively. From data, it could be concluded that NCDs are more efficient for co–sensitization of Pcs over doping compared to that of conjugation due to the higher dispersion of the phthalocyanine dyes. In the case of (NiTCPc)NCDs and NiTCPc-NCDs, the efficiency of the cells was 2.08% and 0.90%, respectively. The efficiency of the cells sensitized by (MnTCPc)NCDs and MnTCPc-NCDs was 1.91% and 0.74%, respectively. From data, the improvement in the efficiency of the solar cells sensitized by (NiTCPc)NCDs and (MnTCPc)NCDs was considerable. The η of the solar cell sensitized by (CoTCPc)NCDs was improved in a noticeable amount relative to (H2TCPc)NCDs, but in the case of (FeTCPc)NCDs and (CuTCPc)NCDs, the improved efficiency of the sensitized cells was relatively low. The mentioned obtained data confirmed the role of the metal in the core of phthalocyanine as well as the phthalocyanine-doping in NCDs on the performance of the solar cells.

There is a race to design new photosensitizers to improve their performance (Cid et al., 2007; Giribabu et al., 2007; Reddy et al., 2007). Many efforts have been made and, different dyes have reported being important, especially phthalocyanines (Cid et al., 2009; Mori et al., 2010; O’Regan et al., 2008). Several symmetric aluminum and zinc phthalocyanines as light-harvesting materials have been studied by Grätzel and co-workers (Nazeeruddin et al., 1999). The effect of the metal center ion in the core of phthalocyanine on the photovoltaic performances has been examined that Zn(II) phthalocyanines performed substantially better than their Al(III) analogues. The effect of the binding group of phthalocyanine on the photovoltaic performances of solar cells has also been studied using the carboxyl- and sulfonic acid-based anchoring groups; the results showed that carboxyl-based dyes outperformed noticeably than sulfonyl analogues. In the present study, the metal in the central cavity altered between manganese (MnPc and MnTCPc), iron (FePc and FeTCPc), cobalt (CoPc and CoTCPc), nickel (NiPc and NiTCPc), copper (CuPc and CuTCPc), and zinc (ZnPc and ZnTCPc). It was found that the metal in the central cavity of the mentioned phthalocyanines is critical toward the photovoltaic performance of Pc-SSCs. In comparison with zinc-based dyes, the performance drastically dropped for the analogues incorporating manganese, iron, cobalt, nickel, and copper in both unsubstituted- and carboxyl-series. Also, the influence of the carboxyl acid (ZnTCPc) and sulfonic acid anchoring groups (ZnTSPc) was compared with the unsubstituted analogue (ZnPc) on the performance of Pc-SSCs. The carboxyl- and sulfonyl-anchored dyes performed better than their unsubstituted analogue due to more efficient attachment and better electronic interaction of ZnTCPc and ZnTSPc on the TiO2 surface. Grätzel and co-workers also used the chenodeoxycholic acid (CHENO) as co-adsorbent and 4-tertbutylpyridine (TBP) as an additive in the electrolyte to improve the performance of the device sensitized with ZnTCPc dye (Nazeeruddin et al., 1999). The overall PCE of 1% achieved for the solar cell sensitized by ZnTCPc in the presence of CHENO and TBP which improved strikingly the photovoltaic performance of the device due to the effect of CHENO and TBP to reduce dye aggregation. In the present study, nitrogen-doped carbon dots were used to reduce dye aggregation by dispersion of dye molecules on the surface of NCDs. The aggregation of the phthalocyanines was decreased notably in the presence of NCDs in the phthalocyanine-NCDs conjugate systems, which considerably improved the JSC for all devices. The increased JSC leads to an increase in the PCEs. In the case of phthalocyanine-doped NCDs systems, the performance of devices considerably improved relative to phthalocyanine-NCDs conjugate analogues. The JSC for all solar cells sensitized by phthalocyanine-doped NCDs systems was higher than their conjugate analogues; that will cause an increment on the observed PCEs.

The poor solubility of phthalocyanines in common organic media makes synthetic procedures and purification of Pcs difficult. It also renders technical difficulties during the fabrication of the DSSC device due to poor solubility of the dye in the dye-uptake solvents. An appropriate dye should have enough solubility in the dye-uptake solvent to be efficiently adsorbed on the metal oxide surface. Also, the high-tendency of phthalocyanines to aggregation affected the efficiency of DSSCs. To address these issues, it has been attempted to improve the molecular design of Pcs. In this way, He and coworkers modified the structure of Pcs by incorporating amino acid groups at the peripheral positions of the Pcs (He et al., 2002). It has been reported that the presence of some amino acid groups promoted the solubility and disaggregation of the phthalocyanine molecules on the TiO2 surface which improved the efficiency of the solar devices. They reported that the solar cell sensitized by phthalocyanine incorporating tyrosine groups displayed a 4-fold improvement of the overall PCE with respect to its analogue with glycine groups. In the present study, the substitutions of phthalocyanine were altered between un-substituted (ZnPc), the carboxyl acid (ZnTCPc), and sulfonic acid substituted (ZnTSPc). Since water is the reaction media for the preparation of phthalocyanine-NCDs conjugates and phthalocyanine-doped NCDs systems, the preparation of the sensitizing systems such as ZnTSPc-NCDs and (ZnTSPc)NCDs was considerably more simple than others from the synthetic procedures and purification viewpoints. The preparation of ZnTCPc-NCDs and (ZnTCPc)NCDs are placed in the second place. Between three dyes such as ZnPc, ZnTCPc, and ZnTSPc for the preparation of phthalocyanine-NCDs conjugates and phthalocyanine-doped NCDs systems, ZnPc is placed in the third place because of the low solubility of the mentioned dye in water. During the fabrication of the Pc-SSCs devices, both of phthalocyanine-NCDs conjugates and phthalocyanine-doped NCDs systems were better than pure phthalocyanine analogues in acetonitrile/tert-butanol as the dye-uptake solvents from the dye solubility and disaggregation points of view.

Today, the design of phthalocyanines and modifying their structure were considered as an important improvement to enhance light harvesting (Giribabu et al., 2013; Kimura et al., 2012; Richards et al., 2012). To improve the efficiency of DSSCs using phthalocyanine dyes, many strategies focused on the molecular design of Pcs. In this way, the effect of the heteroatom bridge has been studied by Sevim and co-workers using substituted phthalocyanines as light harvesters (Sevim et al., 2018). The phthalocyanine core linked through different hetero atoms as S-bridged and/or O-bridged to 4-carboxyphenyl anchoring groups. The photovoltaic performances of devices sensitized by the mentioned substituted Pcs remarkably improved with PCEs of ca. 3–4%. It has been proposed that different heteroatom bridged Pcs had different light-harvesting and electron-injection properties in the solar cells. Many studies have been done on the variation of the peripheral and/or non-peripheral substitution patterns of Pcs to improve the efficiency of the solar devices using the push-pull approach (Ikeuchi et al., 2014; Matsuzaki et al., 2014; Tejerina et al., 2016). In the present work, instead of the design of pull-push systems that need complicated synthesis procedure and those are time and cost consuming, we have used simple and easily accessible Pcs. They can be prepared easily and the improvement of the efficiency of the system was carried out with just using NCDs. The preparation of NCDs is very simple and costly effective. No additive or co-sorbents are needed in our designed system to improve the system.

This report has demonstrated that the performance of phthalocyanine–sensitized solar cells can be dramatically improved by using nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCDs). Two sets of dye systems have been provided to examine the role of NCDs: 1) The phthalocyanine-NCDs conjugate systems were prepared by mixing the related Pcs and NCDs. The obtained results showed that NCDs improve the efficiency of the Pc-SSCs; 2) The phthalocyanine-doped NCDs were prepared by in situ synthesis of NCDs in the presence of the related Pcs. The cell parameters measurements showed that NCDs markedly improve the efficiency of the solar cells by doping the Pcs in the NCDs. The overall conversion efficiency values obtained using phthalocyanine-doped NCDs were different from the related phthalocyanine-NCDs conjugates. The overall conversion efficiency enhancement is dependent on the JSC and VOC values. The lower overall conversion efficiency values for the solar cells sensitized by phthalocyanine-NCDs conjugates is probably due to a downward shift of their JSC values relative to the phthalocyanine-doped NCDs. The upward shift of the JSC values of the solar cells sensitized by phthalocyanine-doped NCDs may occur because of the higher dispersion of phthalocyanine in the NCDs over in situ synthesis of NCDs in the presence of phthalocyanine. Such that the co-sensitization of dyes using NCDs in the phthalocyanine-doped NCDs is done better than that of the phthalocyanine-NCDs conjugates. The obtained results prove the feasibility of the co-sensitization of phthalocyanine dyes using NCDs which effectively increase the efficiency of the DSSCs. The low solubility of the phthalocyanines such as H2Pc, MnPc, FePc, CoPc, NiPc, CuPc, and ZnPc in water is the most important limitation of the suggested method to prepare phthalocyanine-NCDs conjugate systems and phthalocyanine-doped NCDs. The mentioned limitation significantly was overcome by using the functionalized phthalocyanines such as H2TCPc, MnTCPc, FeTCPc, CoTCPc, NiTCPc, CuTCPc, and ZnTCPc.

Rahim Ghadari: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Investigation, Project administration, Writing - original draft. Paria-Sadat Saei: Investigation, Methodology, Data curation. Alireza Sabri: Investigation, Methodology, Data curation. Zarrin Ghasemi: Formal analysis. Fantai Kong: Writing - review & editing.
